examples.title: Algorithm: SCAN Disk Scheduling
examples.description: Demonstrates the SCAN disk scheduling algorithm which moves the disk arm across the full width of the disk, servicing requests in order of increasing/decreasing track number
examples.code:
ALGORITHM SCAN(requests, start_track, disk_size)
INPUT: List of track requests, starting position, total disk tracks
OUTPUT: Order of serviced requests and total seek time

BEGIN
 SET seek_count = 0
 SET current_track = start_track
 SET left = [] // requests to the left of start
 SET right = [] // requests to the right of start
 SET sequence = [] // final service order

 // Step 1: Separate requests into left and right of start
 FOR each request IN requests:
 IF request < start_track:
 ADD request TO left
 ELSE:
 ADD request TO right
 END FOR

 // Step 2: Sort both lists
 SORT left IN DESCENDING order
 SORT right IN ASCENDING order

 // Step 3: Service right side first (towards end of disk)
 FOR each request IN right:
 seek_count = seek_count + ABS(current_track - request)
 current_track = request
 ADD request TO sequence
 END FOR

 // Step 4: Go to end of disk (maximum seek)
 seek_count = seek_count + ABS(current_track - (disk_size - 1))
 current_track = disk_size - 1

 // Step 5: Service left side (reverse direction)
 FOR each request IN left:
 seek_count = seek_count + ABS(current_track - request)
 current_track = request
 ADD request TO sequence
 END FOR

 OUTPUT sequence, seek_count
 RETURN sequence
END
examples.explanation: The SCAN algorithm (also known as the elevator algorithm) works like an elevator - it moves in one direction servicing all requests until it reaches the end, then reverses direction. This example shows: 1) Requests are separated into those less than and greater than the starting position 2) Right side is serviced first (moving toward disk edge) 3) Arm goes to the end of disk 4) Then reverses to service remaining requests on the left. This reduces average seek time compared to FCFS.

examples.title: Python: LRU Cache Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates Least Recently Used (LRU) cache with hit/miss tracking and cache coherency concept
examples.code:
from collections import OrderedDict

class LRUCache:
 """
 Least Recently Used (LRU) Cache Implementation
 Demonstrates caching principles in storage management
 """
 
 def __init__(self, capacity):
 self.capacity = capacity
 self.cache = OrderedDict() # OrderedDict maintains insertion order
 self.hits = 0
 self.misses = 0
 
 def access(self, key):
 """
 Access data from cache
 Returns: (data, hit_status)
 """
 if key in self.cache:
 # Cache hit: move to end (most recently used)
 self.hits += 1
 self.cache.move_to_end(key)
 return self.cache[key], True
 else:
 # Cache miss: load from main memory (simulated)
 self.misses += 1
 data = self._load_from_memory(key)
 
 # If cache is full, remove least recently used
 if len(self.cache) >= self.capacity:
 # Remove oldest item (first item)
 self.cache.popitem(last=False)
 
 self.cache[key] = data
 return data, False
 
 def _load_from_memory(self, key):
 """Simulate loading data from main memory"""
 # Simulate slower main memory access
 return f"Data_{key}_from_Memory"
 
 def write(self, key, value):
 """
 Write data to cache (write-through policy)
 Demonstrates cache coherency concept
 """
 # Write-through: update both cache and main memory
 self.cache[key] = value
 if len(self.cache) >= self.capacity:
 self.cache.popitem(last=False)
 
 # In write-through, we would also update main memory here
 self._write_to_memory(key, value)
 
 def _write_to_memory(self, key, value):
 """Simulate writing to main memory"""
 pass # Would write to main memory in real implementation
 
 def get_stats(self):
 """Return cache performance statistics"""
 total = self.hits + self.misses
 hit_rate = (self.hits / total * 100) if total > 0 else 0
 return {
 "hits": self.hits,
 "misses": self.misses,
 "hit_rate": f"{hit_rate:.2f}%"
 }


# Demonstration
print("=== LRU Cache Simulation ===\n")

# Create cache with capacity of 3 frames
cache = LRUCache(capacity=3)

# Simulate memory accesses
accesses = [1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

print("Accessing data in order:", accesses)
print("\nAccess Results:")
for key in accesses:
 data, is_hit = cache.access(key)
 status = "HIT" if is_hit else "MISS"
 print(f" Key {key}: {status} -> {data}")

print("\nCache Statistics:", cache.get_stats())
print("\nFinal Cache State (most recent at end):", list(cache.cache.keys()))
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates key caching concepts: 1) LRU eviction policy removes least recently used items when cache is full 2) Cache hits vs misses tracking 3) Write-through policy for cache coherency (data written to both cache and main memory) 4) The OrderedDict maintains access order efficiently. The simulation shows how caching improves performance by keeping frequently accessed data in fast storage.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for access and write operations

examples.title: C: First-Fit Memory Allocation
examples.description: Demonstrates contiguous memory allocation using First-Fit algorithm for managing main memory
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates First-Fit contiguous memory allocation: 1) Memory is managed as blocks with start address and size 2) First-Fit algorithm finds the first free block large enough for the request 3) When a block is larger than needed, it's split into allocated and free portions 4) Coalescing merges adjacent free blocks when memory is deallocated 5) Demonstrates both allocation and fragmentation issues. This shows how OS manages main memory for processes.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for allocation, O(n) for deallocation where n is number of blocks
